content.problemStatement:
A tree is defined as a connected, undirected graph with nnn vertices and n−1n−1n−1 edges. The distance between two vertices in a tree is equal to the number of edges on the unique simple path between them.

You are given two integers xxx and yyy. Construct a tree with the following properties:


The number of pairs of vertices with an **even** distance between them equals xxx.
The number of pairs of vertices with an **odd** distance between them equals yyy.
By a pair of vertices, we mean an ordered pair of two (possibly, the same or different) vertices.
constraints: `ExplanationIn the first test case,  the pairs (1,1)(1 ,1)(1,1) and (2,2)(2 ,2)(2,2) have an even distance, while the pairs (1,2)(1 ,2)(1,2) and (2,1)(2 ,1)(2,1) have an odd distance.
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. Each test case consists of one line containing two space-separated integers xxx and yyy.
content.outputFormat:
For each test case, if there is no tree satisfying the given properties, print `"NO"` (without quotes).

Otherwise, on the first line print `"YES"` (without quotes). Then print integer nnn denoting the number of vertices in the tree, followed by n−1n−1n−1 lines describing the edges of the tree in any order. Vertices are numbered from 111 to nnn. If there are multiple answers, print any of them.
